title: Do I Need Glasses? Signs, Self-Checks, and When to See an Optometrist
description: Wondering if you need glasses? Learn the common signs of vision problems in adults and kids, simple self-checks, and why an eye exam is the only way to know for sure.
tldr: Common signs you might need glasses: squinting to see clearly, headaches after reading or screen work, difficulty with night driving, holding your phone farther away, and eye fatigue. Online vision tests can hint at a problem but can't replace a comprehensive eye exam. In Canada, most insurance plans cover exams, and kids under 18 get coverage through provincial health plans.

definition.text: A condition where the eye cannot focus light correctly on the retina, resulting in blurred vision. The four types are myopia (nearsightedness), hyperopia (farsightedness), astigmatism (irregular corneal curvature), and presbyopia (age-related loss of near focus). All are correctable with glasses, contacts, or surgery.

sections.content:
Squinting: You catch yourself squinting at distant signs, screens, or your phone. Squinting partially closes the eye's aperture, temporarily improving focus — but it's a sign your natural lens isn't doing the job.

Headaches: Frequent headaches, especially after reading, screen work, or driving, often stem from eye strain caused by uncorrected vision. Your eye muscles overwork to compensate for blur, and the resulting tension radiates into headaches.

Difficulty with distance or near vision: Trouble reading road signs (myopia), difficulty reading small text (hyperopia or presbyopia), or blurred vision that seems to fluctuate can all indicate a need for correction.

Eye fatigue: Your eyes feel tired, heavy, or strained, especially by evening. This is different from dry eyes (though they often coexist) — fatigue is the exhaustion of focusing muscles that are overcompensating.

Night driving problems: Halos around lights, difficulty judging distances, and generally worse vision after dark can indicate uncorrected astigmatism or myopia that your brain compensates for in good lighting.

sections.h2: Signs Your Child Might Need Glasses
sections.content:
Children rarely complain about blurry vision because they don't know what normal looks like. Watch for these behavioural signs:

  • Sitting too close to the TV or holding devices very close to their face
  • Frequent eye rubbing (beyond tiredness)
  • Tilting or turning their head to see clearly
  • Losing their place while reading or using a finger to track lines
  • Avoiding reading or close work
  • One eye drifting or crossing (this is urgent — see an optometrist immediately)
  • Declining school performance, especially in subjects requiring board reading

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends a first eye exam at 6 months, again before starting school, and annually through childhood. In Alberta, children's eye exams are covered by provincial health insurance.

If you're experiencing any of these signs regularly, it's worth getting a professional assessment rather than guessing. Many vision problems develop gradually — your brain compensates for declining vision so effectively that you may not notice the change until it's significant. A comprehensive eye exam can detect issues that self-assessment misses, including early signs of conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration that have no symptoms in their early stages.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults. Children should be examined annually since vision problems can significantly impact learning and development. In Alberta, children under 18 receive coverage through Alberta Health. Adults with diabetes or a family history of eye disease should also get annual exams. Book an appointment at Charm Optical — they accept most Alberta insurance providers through direct billing.

sections.h2: Self-Checks You Can Do Right Now
sections.content:
These aren't substitutes for a real exam, but they can help you decide if booking one is worthwhile:

Distance test: Can you read licence plates clearly from 20 metres? Street signs from a block away? If you're squinting or unsure, that's informative.

Near test: Hold a book or phone at your normal reading distance. Is the text sharp without effort? Now slowly move it closer — does it blur? If comfortable reading distance keeps getting farther away, you may be developing presbyopia.

Astigmatism check: Look at a clock face or a grid of lines from across the room. Do some lines appear darker or sharper than others? Astigmatism causes directional blur — horizontal might be clear while vertical is soft, or vice versa.

Cover test: Look at a distant object and cover one eye, then the other. Does the image shift dramatically? If so, you may have a binocular vision issue worth investigating.

All of these are rough indicators. Only a comprehensive eye exam with proper instruments can measure your actual vision and eye health.

sections.h2: What Happens During an Eye Exam
sections.content:
A comprehensive eye exam takes about 20–30 minutes and includes:

  • Visual acuity test: Reading letters on a chart at distance and near — the classic "which is better, 1 or 2?" refraction
  • Refraction: Precisely measuring your prescription using a phoropter (the device with all the lenses you look through)
  • Eye health examination: Checking for glaucoma, cataracts, retinal problems, and other conditions using specialized instruments
  • Binocular vision assessment: Testing how your eyes work together — focusing, tracking, and alignment
  • Intraocular pressure: Measuring pressure inside the eye (a glaucoma screening)

The exam is painless. Dilation drops may be used (they blur your near vision for a few hours), but many modern instruments can evaluate the retina without them. Your optometrist will explain every finding in plain language.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

In Alberta, provincial health coverage (AHCIP) covers comprehensive eye exams for children under 18 and adults over 65. For adults 18–64, coverage is limited to specific medical conditions.

Most employer health insurance plans cover annual or biennial eye exams for adults. Common Alberta insurance providers like Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and Great-West Life typically include eye exam benefits.

Even without insurance, an eye exam in Edmonton typically costs $100–$150 — a modest investment considering it checks both your vision and your eye health. Early detection of conditions like glaucoma can prevent irreversible vision loss.

If you're wondering whether you need glasses, the simplest next step is to book an eye exam. In 30 minutes, you'll have a definitive answer and, if needed, a prescription you can use to get glasses that actually match your eyes.

Buying glasses online might save money upfront, but the fit and vision quality often suffer without professional measurements. Your pupillary distance (PD), segment height for progressive lenses, and frame alignment all need to be measured precisely. Even a 2mm error in PD can cause eye strain, headaches, and blurry vision — especially with progressive or high-prescription lenses.

An experienced optician also considers factors that online tools can't measure: how the frame sits on your nose bridge, whether the temples create pressure behind your ears, and how the lens angle affects your vision. These adjustments are the difference between glasses you love wearing and glasses that sit in a drawer.

At Charm Optical in Edmonton, every pair of glasses includes a professional fitting at no extra charge. The opticians use digital measurement tools for precision and take the time to ensure your frames are comfortable and your lenses are optimally positioned. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses at Charm Optical for many prescriptions, getting properly fitted glasses doesn't have to be expensive or slow. book an eye exam online to get started.

faqs.a: Yes. Vision changes can happen gradually (you don't notice until it's significant) or relatively quickly (hormonal changes, diabetes, certain medications). Presbyopia — the need for reading glasses — develops noticeably in your early to mid-40s and feels quite sudden even though it's been progressing for years.

faqs.q: Do I need glasses if I only have trouble seeing at night?
faqs.a: Possibly. Night vision problems often indicate mild myopia or astigmatism that your brain compensates for in good lighting but can't overcome in the dark. An eye exam will determine if glasses would help, especially for driving.

faqs.q: How often should I get an eye exam?
faqs.a: The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends annually for children and seniors, and every 1–2 years for adults with no known issues. More frequently if you have diabetes, a family history of eye disease, or notice vision changes.

faqs.q: Can wearing glasses make your eyes worse?
faqs.a: No. This is a common myth. Glasses correct your vision but don't change the underlying eye structure. What happens is that once you experience clear vision with glasses, your brain notices the blur without them more than it did before — but your eyes haven't gotten worse.

Online screening tools can flag potential issues but cannot replace a comprehensive eye exam. They can't measure your prescription precisely, check eye pressure, examine the retina, or assess binocular vision. Use them as motivation to book a real exam, not as a substitute.
